mcqs.question: For an array of 1000 elements, what is the maximum number of comparisons required in the worst case for Linear Search?
mcqs.explanation: Linear Search in worst case examines every element until finding the target or reaching the end. For 1000 elements, worst-case requires 1000 comparisons when the element is at the last position or not present at all. This is O(n) complexity.

mcqs.question: Given a sorted array of 1024 elements, what is the maximum number of comparisons needed to find an element using Binary Search?
mcqs.explanation: Binary Search halves the search space each iteration. For n=1024, maximum iterations = log₂1024 = log₂2¹⁰ = 10. This is why binary search has O(log n) complexity — exponentially better than linear search's O(n).

mcqs.question: Consider inserting an element at position 0 in an array of size n. What is the time complexity?
mcqs.explanation: Inserting at the beginning of an array requires shifting all n existing elements one position to the right. This shifting operation takes Θ(n) time in the worst case. The actual insertion itself is O(1), but element movement dominates.

mcqs.question: What is the recurrence relation for the worst-case time complexity of Merge Sort and what is its solution?

options.text: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, solution: O(n log n)

mcqs.explanation: Merge Sort divides the array into two halves (2T(n/2)), recursively sorts each half, then merges them in O(n) time. Applying Master's Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. Since log₂2=1, we have f(n)=Θ(n¹), so T(n)=Θ(n¹ log n)=O(n log n).

mcqs.question: Two sorted arrays of sizes 500 and 700 are merged. What is the time complexity of this merge operation?
mcqs.explanation: The merge operation compares elements from both arrays and places them in order. For arrays of sizes m and n, merge takes O(m+n) time. Here m+n = 500+700 = 1200. Each element is processed exactly once in the worst case.

mcqs.question: In a singly linked list with a pointer to the head, what is the time complexity to insert a new node at the beginning?
mcqs.explanation: Insertion at the beginning of a linked list requires creating a new node, setting its next pointer to the current head, and updating the head pointer. All these operations are constant time O(1), independent of list size. This is a key advantage over array insertion at the beginning which requires O(n) shifts.

mcqs.question: If the recurrence relation for an algorithm is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), what is the time complexity using Master's Theorem?
mcqs.explanation: Here a=1, b=2, f(n)=1. log_b(a) = log₂1 = 0. Since f(n) = Θ(n^0) = Θ(1), we have case 2 of Master's Theorem: T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) × log n) = Θ(1 × log n) = Θ(log n). This is the complexity of Binary Search.

mcqs.question: What is the average number of comparisons required to search for an element in a sorted array of 100 elements using Linear Search, assuming the element is equally likely to be at any position?
mcqs.explanation: For linear search, average comparisons = (1+2+3+...+n)/n = n(n+1)/(2n) = (n+1)/2. For n=100, average = 101/2 = 50.5 comparisons. This assumes uniform distribution and element is always present.

mcqs.question: Which data structure operation has O(1) time complexity for both insertion and deletion at the front?
mcqs.explanation: A singly linked list provides O(1) insertion and deletion at the head (front) because it only requires updating the head pointer. In contrast, arrays require O(n) shifting for front operations. Note: Deletion at tail is O(n) for singly linked list without tail pointer.

mcqs.question: For a hash table with n elements and m slots using linear probing, what is the worst-case time complexity for search?
mcqs.explanation: In linear probing, worst-case occurs when there is primary clustering — all elements cluster into one long run. Searching may need to traverse almost all slots. In the worst case (full table or severe clustering), time complexity degrades to O(n).

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of the recursive merge sort algorithm for n elements?
mcqs.explanation: While merge sort has time complexity O(n log n), it requires an auxiliary array of size n for merging. Therefore, space complexity is O(n). The recursion depth is O(log n), but the dominant space is the temporary array used during merge.

mcqs.question: A doubly linked list has 50 nodes. What is the minimum number of node traversals needed to find a node if we only have a pointer to the tail and the target is at position 25 from the beginning?
mcqs.explanation: With only a tail pointer and knowing the target is at position 25 from head (i.e., position 25 from tail as well, since 50-25+1=26 from tail for 1-indexed), we must traverse from tail backward. Since position from head = 25, position from tail = 50-25+1 = 26. Minimum = 25 traversals from tail to reach position 26 (or equivalently 25 positions back).

mcqs.question: Which operation is NOT typically classified as one of the six fundamental data structure operations?
mcqs.explanation: The six fundamental operations on data structures are: Traversal, Searching, Insertion, Deletion, Sorting, and Merging. Encryption is a security operation unrelated to basic data structure manipulation. It transforms data for security rather than organizing or accessing it.

mcqs.question: In a circular linked list with 100 nodes, how many nodes are visited in a complete traversal starting from any node?
mcqs.explanation: A complete traversal of a circular linked list visits every node exactly once and returns to the starting node. With 100 nodes, exactly 100 nodes are visited during the traversal (the return to start is not counted as a separate visit). This holds regardless of starting position.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity to delete the k-th element from an array of size n (where k < n)?
mcqs.explanation: Deletion from an array requires: (1) Removing the element at index k, and (2) shifting all elements from position k+1 to n-1 one position left. In worst case (deleting at position 0), n-1 elements must shift. This is O(n) time complexity. The exact shift count is n-k elements.
